segments.title: Royal Navy 4.7-inch dual-purpose counterfactual
segments.summary: The episode argues that a true high-angle 4.7-inch Royal Navy gun could have simplified armament logistics and strengthened early-war fleet air defence.
segments.body: The discussion captures the lecture's organizing counterfactual. Dr. Clarke narrows the premise to developing the existing 4.7-inch 45-calibre gun into a true dual-purpose weapon after the failed 5-inch project, then traces how a standardized mount could affect destroyers, carriers, cruisers, capital ships, and operations exposed to air attack.

evidence.note: Frames the change as replacing the 4.5-inch path with development of the existing 4.7-inch 45-calibre gun into a true dual-purpose system.
Identifies affected battleships, carriers, cruisers, and AA cruiser conversions if the high-angle 4.7-inch mount existed.
Argues the aggregate effect would be stronger early-war air defence, fewer aircraft-attack successes, and wider replacement of 4-inch and 4.5-inch fits.
Explains why the Royal Navy still pursued specialist 4-inch and 4.5-inch solutions despite the counterfactual benefits.
